text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Eleanor Harris  
St George’s University Hospital  
Department of Neurology, Blackshaw Road, London SW17 0QT  

Dear Dr Eleanor Harris,  

Alex Morgan, DOB: 14-03-1991, Hospital No: H392847 NHS No. 1045582237  
12 Ainsworth Close, London, SW18 2LX 

Diagnoses: 

Recurrent seizures of uncertain classification since 2023. MRI brain (June 2024) unremarkable. Ambulatory EEG (August 2024) no definite epileptiform discharges. 

Current Medication: 

Levetiracetam 500 mg bd  
Vitamin D3 1000 IU od 

Plan: 

- Continue Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily.  
- Maintain seizure diary with explicit weekly counts; currently documented as “seizure frequency four/7”.  
- Safety advice reinforced, including SUDEP discussion, water safety, and use of assistance dog in training.  
- DVLA guidance discussed; patient self-reports abstaining from driving.  
- Referral to epilepsy specialist nurse for education and rescue plan review.  
- Consider dose optimisation or alternative agent if frequency persists beyond three months despite adherence.  

I reviewed Alex Morgan in clinic today. They report ongoing brief loss-of-awareness events with occasional lip-smacking and post-event confusion. There have been no injuries, tongue bites, or incontinence reported. Triggers described include sleep deprivation and high work-related stress. A structured diary was brought to the appointment; over the last two weeks the entries document “seizure frequency four/7”, which the patient clarified as approximately four events per week, with clustering towards the end of the working week. 

Since commencing Levetiracetam six months ago, the duration of events appears shorter and recovery quicker, though frequency remains as recorded above. No significant mood change, irritability, or somatic side effects were reported. Adherence is reported as good with use of a pill organiser and phone reminders. 

The patient keeps an assistance dog in training, which they feel provides early alerting to behavioural changes and aids post-event grounding. We discussed appropriate public access etiquette, maintaining identification for the dog, and ensuring that the handler’s safety strategies do not rely solely on the animal. Safety measures at home include avoidance of bathing (showers only), use of a kettle instead of open pans when alone, and fall-proofing of sharp furniture edges. 

We reviewed red flags warranting urgent assessment (prolonged events over 5 minutes, repeated events without full recovery, major injury, or a change in semiology). They have rescue buccal midazolam available at home but have not needed to use it. 

I will re-review in three months, sooner if the pattern worsens despite adherence. Please contact me if there are concerns regarding mood, adherence, or evolving semiology. 

With best wishes,

entities.text: entries document “seizure frequency four/7”, which the patient clarified as approximately four events per week

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter states the patient maintains a diary with explicit weekly counts and that entries over the last two weeks document “seizure frequency four/7”, clarified by the patient as approximately four events per week. No other conflicting frequencies are provided. Therefore, the highest/current frequency is 4 per week.
